qa.question: Which statement best matches Dr. Stockmann's fundamental attitude toward the established beliefs of the masses?
option: B. He respects their old age.
C. He views them as outdated and morally corrupting lies.
D. He believes they are mostly true but need minor updates.
E. He completely agrees with the compact majority and wants to conform to their established social norms.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The majority never has right on its side. Never, I say! That is one of these social lies against which an independent, intelligent man must wage war.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: These "majority truths" are like last year's cured meat—like rancid, tainted ham; and they are the origin of the moral scurvy that is rampant in our communities.

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann identifies the majority's views as social lies that must be fought.
He explicitly equates these accepted truths to tainted meat causing moral disease, confirming his hostile and critical attitude toward them.

qa.question: Which option reflects Hovstad's professional philosophy regarding his newspaper?
option: B. He aligns with the majority.
C. He fights against the town's Mayor.
D. He ignores the ratepayers completely.
E. He aggressively promotes revolutionary ideas regardless of what his local subscribers think.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: But the advice of experienced and thoughtful men has convinced me that in purely local matters a newspaper ought to proceed with a certain caution.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Now, what is an editor's first and most obvious duty, gentlemen? Is it not to work in harmony with his readers?

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad believes newspapers must be cautious in local matters.
He explicitly defines his primary duty as working in harmony with his readers (the majority), establishing his alignment with public opinion.

qa.question: Which statement best describes Katherine's primary focus during the public meeting?
option: C. She wants to lead the crowd.
D. She tries to manage her family's safety and decorum.
E. She actively attempts to deliver her own lecture to the hostile citizens gathered in the room.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: All right, thank you. Be sure not to lose your temper, Thomas.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Thomas, dear, let us go out by the back way.

reasoning_steps.inference: Mrs. Stockmann advises her husband to control his temper, showing a desire for decorum.
After the meeting turns completely hostile, she suggests taking the back exit, indicating her focus on physical safety and avoiding conflict.

qa.question: What is the Mayor's consistent approach to handling his brother's opposition?
option: C. He leverages procedure and public expense fears to block him.
D. He begs him to speak longer to humiliate him publicly.
E. He completely ignores the situation and allows the Medical Officer to say whatever he wants to the crowd.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: From it you will see that the main result of the Medical Officer's proposals... would be to saddle the ratepayers with an unnecessary expenditure of at least some thousands of pounds.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Therefore, I should like to propose that the meeting should not permit the Medical Officer either to read or to comment on his proposed lecture.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor uses the financial threat to the ratepayers as a weapon against his brother.
He formally uses meeting procedure to propose a gag order on the Doctor.

qa.question: How does Petra generally react to those involved in her father's struggle?
option: B. She praises those who are brave.
C. She joins the crowd in hissing.
D. She physically fights the townspeople.
E. She publicly denounces her father and immediately leaves the meeting hall to go back to her house alone.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And it was a plucky thing to do, Captain Horster.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Horster
evidence_dialogues.utterance: For you and yours we will make room, Doctor.

reasoning_steps.inference: Petra openly admires Horster's courage in giving them the room.
Her positive alignment is consistently with those who provide brave support, like Horster.

qa.question: What is Aslaksen's defining principle in public affairs?
option: D. Discretion, moderation, and protecting the ratepayers.
E. Advocating for the immediate and total destruction of all existing local government structures and town administration.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I have learned in the school of life and experience that moderation is the most valuable virtue a citizen can possess—

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I myself am a believer in self-government for the people, provided it does not fall too heavily on the ratepayers.

reasoning_steps.inference: Aslaksen explicitly identifies moderation as the highest virtue.
He links his political beliefs directly to protecting the financial interests of the ratepayers.

qa.question: How does Dr. Stockmann's emotional state shift at the beginning of the meeting?
option: C. He moves from claiming self-control to fiery indignation.
D. He is consistently sleepy and entirely bored by the crowd.
E. He instantly attacks the Mayor physically and destroys the furniture on the platform.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Oh, I know how to control myself.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I should like to have seen anyone, a few days ago, dare to attempt to silence me as has been done tonight! I would have defended my sacred rights as a man, like a lion!

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann initially assures his wife he is composed.
Once gagged by the vote, his speech immediately erupts into passionate, indignant defiance.

qa.question: What is Hovstad's immediate motivation for speaking up during the initial debate?
option: B. He feels the need to justify his betrayal.
C. He wants to physically protect the Mayor.
D. He is trying to win Petra's affection immediately.
E. He intends to forcefully disband the entire meeting and throw everyone out into the street.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I, too, feel called upon to explain my position.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: It has cost me a severe struggle to break with a man in whose house I have been lately a frequent guest...

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad explicitly states he feels compelled to explain himself.
His explanation centers on framing his abandonment of his friend as a difficult but necessary duty.

qa.question: What is Katherine's short-term reaction when her family is brought into the public argument?
option: C. She starts a formal debate.
D. She quickly shushes her son to avoid attention.
E. She runs onto the stage and violently attacks the editor for mentioning her children.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: consideration, I repeat, for his wife and his children for whom he has made no provision.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Is that us, mother?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hush!

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad publicly invokes the Doctor's family.
When Morten reacts, Katherine immediately silences him, showing her desire to remain unnoticed in the tense atmosphere.

qa.question: What immediate goal drives Peter Stockmann's interruptions during the Doctor's speech?
option: C. To censor and control the Medical Officer's language.
D. To resign his position as the Mayor of the town.
E. To permanently rewrite the entire legal code of the town based on the new discoveries.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: ...what I should like best would be to see them exterminated like any other vermin—.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Mr. Chairman, can we allow such expressions to pass?

reasoning_steps.inference: The Doctor uses inflammatory language about leading men.
The Mayor immediately appeals to the chair to stop this specific language, showing his goal is active censorship.

qa.question: What immediate concern does Petra express near the end of the meeting?
option: B. Identifying the man who just threatened their host.
D. Securing a new job at the newspaper.
E. Organizing a brand new political party to completely overthrow the local government.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: The Stout Man
evidence_dialogues.utterance: You shall hear from me in the morning.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Was that not your owner, Captain Horster?

reasoning_steps.inference: A Stout Man threatens Horster.
Petra immediately asks to identify the man, focusing on the real-time threat to their ally.

qa.question: How does Aslaksen handle the final vote process?
option: D. He swiftly organizes a silent ballot to avoid further emotional outbursts.
E. He demands that every single citizen speak for an hour before casting their final paper.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: We cannot hear you now, Doctor. A formal vote is about to be taken; but, out of regard for personal feelings, it shall be by ballot and not verbal.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: That will do nicely; we shall get on more quickly that way.

reasoning_steps.inference: Aslaksen silences the Doctor to push the vote.
He explicitly chooses paper ballots to speed things up and manage personal feelings.

qa.question: What is the chronological sequence of Dr. Stockmann's realizations about his town?
option: B. He hatched the plans yesterday.
C. He planned the baths up north, then realized the truth yesterday afternoon.
D. He realized the truth as a child, then forgot it.
E. He learned the truth from his brother ten years ago and kept it a total secret until this evening.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I was like one of the eider-ducks brooding on its nest, and what I hatched was the plans for these Baths.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: But yesterday morning—no, to be precise, it was yesterday afternoon—the eyes of my mind were opened wide, and the first thing I realised was the colossal stupidity of the authorities—.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Doctor first created the plans in the past (up north).
His new revelation about the authorities and the truth occurred specifically 'yesterday afternoon'.

qa.question: What event caused Hovstad to sequentially change his mind about supporting the Doctor?
option: B. He read the Mayor's statement.
C. He was bribed by Morten Kiil.
D. He lost his job at the newspaper.
E. He received a secret telegram from the capital ordering him to change his political stance immediately.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Dr. Stockmann's agitation appeared to be gaining a certain amount of sympathy at first, so I supported it as impartially as I could.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Well, let us say a not entirely trustworthy representation. The Mayor's statement has proved that.

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad supported the Doctor earlier in the timeline.
He justifies his shift by claiming the Mayor's statement proved the Doctor's representation was untrustworthy.

qa.question: What is the sequence of Mrs. Stockmann's logistical concerns during the event?
option: D. She asked about a disturbance upon arrival, and later suggested a back exit.
E. She demanded to be seated on the main platform but was subsequently thrown out of the room.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Do you think there will be a disturbance?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Thomas, dear, let us go out by the back way.

reasoning_steps.inference: Early in the scene, Katherine worries about a disturbance upon arriving.
After the hostility peaks at the end, she sequentially proposes using the back way to leave.

qa.question: What is the chronological order of the Mayor's actions against his brother?
option: C. He published in the Messenger, then proposed the gag order.
D. He fought him physically before writing.
E. He voted secretly and then refused to speak to anyone in the town for the rest of the year.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: In my communication to the "People's Messenger," I have put the essential facts before the public...

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Therefore, I should like to propose that the meeting should not permit the Medical Officer either to read or to comment on his proposed lecture.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor references a past action: his communication already published in the paper.
His present action is to propose the motion blocking the lecture.

qa.question: How do Petra's interactions with Horster evolve from the start to the end of the meeting?
option: B. She praises him initially, then later identifies his threatening boss.
C. She argues with him constantly.
D. She begs him for money.
E. She demands he sell his ship and move away from the town immediately to avoid further trouble.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And it was a plucky thing to do, Captain Horster.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Was that not your owner, Captain Horster?

reasoning_steps.inference: At the beginning, Petra praises Horster for hosting.
Near the end, she actively identifies the Stout Man as Horster's owner after a threat.

qa.question: What is the sequence of Aslaksen's official duties during this scene?
option: D. He accepted the chairmanship, stated his principles, and later dissolved the meeting.
E. He completely refused to participate in any voting and locked himself inside the adjacent room.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Since my fellow-citizens choose to entrust me with this duty, I cannot refuse.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And now, as I am in this position, I should like to say a few brief words... moderation is the most valuable virtue...

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The meeting is dissolved.

reasoning_steps.inference: Aslaksen first accepts the role and states his doctrine of moderation.
At the very end of the chronological sequence, he officially dissolves the gathering.

qa.question: What major plot event occurs after Dr. Stockmann is prevented from reading his lecture?
option: C. He delivers a speech denouncing the compact majority.
D. He writes a new book.
E. He explicitly apologizes to everyone in the room and promises to never mention the poisoned water again.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Tonight I have no intention of dealing with all that filth down at the Baths. No; I have something quite different to say to you.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The most dangerous enemy of truth and freedom amongst us is the compact majority...

reasoning_steps.inference: Prevented from discussing the Baths, the Doctor pivots to a different topic.
He executes his new plan by delivering a speech targeting the compact majority.

qa.question: What specific action does Hovstad take to solidify his stance at the meeting?
option: B. He uses his speech to publicly denounce his former friend.
E. He forcibly removes the Doctor from the platform and burns his manuscript in front of the crowd.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: It has cost me a severe struggle to break with a man in whose house I have been lately a frequent guest...

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: But my duty to the community obliged me to break with him.

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad actively takes the floor to address the crowd.
He officially and publicly breaks ties with the Doctor during this speech.

qa.question: What physical action does Katherine take when the debate touches upon her family?
option: D. She hushes her son Morten.
E. She stands up and delivers a prolonged defense of her husband's medical qualifications to the entire crowd.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Is that us, mother?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hush!

reasoning_steps.inference: Morten speaks out when his family is mentioned.
Katherine executes the action of shushing him.

qa.question: What maneuver does the Mayor execute to ensure the meeting goes his way?
option: C. He nominates Aslaksen as chairman.
E. He permanently locks the doors of the hall so that no one can enter or leave until the vote is finished.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I certainly think too that we ought to have a chairman.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I refer to the President of the Householders' Association, Mr. Aslaksen.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor insists on procedural control via a chairman.
He directly nominates the moderate Aslaksen, ensuring the meeting remains under his faction's control.

qa.question: What is Petra tasked with as the family prepares to leave the hostile meeting?
option: B. She is asked to bring her father's hat and coat.
D. She fights the drunken man.
E. She is commanded to stay behind and clean the entire room after the crowd has completely dispersed.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The meeting is dissolved.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: My hat and coat, Petra! Captain, have you room on your ship for passengers to the New World?

reasoning_steps.inference: The meeting ends abruptly after the hostile vote.
Dr. Stockmann directly tasks Petra with fetching his hat and coat for their departure.

qa.question: How does Aslaksen practically execute the resolution against Dr. Stockmann?
option: D. By cutting up blue and white paper strips for a ballot.
E. By forcing every single person to sign a massive legal document permanently banishing the Doctor.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: A formal vote is about to be taken... it shall be by ballot...

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Cut it up into small strips—yes, that's it. Blue means no; white means yes.

reasoning_steps.inference: Aslaksen calls for a formal ballot.
He executes this by directing the cutting of blue and white paper strips.

qa.question: What is the explicit familial relationship between the speaker attempting to give the lecture and the Mayor?
option: D. They are father and son.
E. They have absolutely no biological or legal relation to one another whatsoever.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: 4th Citizen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: But the Mayor is his brother.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: ...I had almost daily before my eyes in this town such an excellent specimen of them—my brother Peter—

reasoning_steps.inference: The crowd notes the relationship before the speech.
The Doctor explicitly confirms Peter is his brother.

qa.question: What explicit prior relationship did Hovstad have with the speaker's household?
option: B. He was a frequent guest in his house.
C. He was his medical patient.
D. He was a business partner.
E. He was completely unacquainted with the family until the very moment the public meeting began.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: It has cost me a severe struggle to break with a man in whose house I have been lately a frequent guest...

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad explicitly defines his relationship as having been a frequent guest in Dr. Stockmann's house.

qa.question: What is Katherine's explicit familial relationship to Morten?
option: E. She is an entirely unrelated guardian who was hired to watch him during the meeting.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Morten
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Is that us, mother?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hush!

reasoning_steps.inference: Morten directly addresses Mrs. Stockmann as 'mother'.
Her immediate response confirms she occupies this maternal role.

qa.question: What is Peter Stockmann's explicit relationship to Dr. Thomas Stockmann?
option: E. He is a completely unrelated official who happens to share the exact same last name.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: But I have called this meeting to deliver a lecture, Peter.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: ...my magnificent brother Peter...

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann refers to the Mayor as Peter.
Dr. Stockmann explicitly names Peter as his brother.

qa.question: How is Petra explicitly related to the Medical Officer?
option: E. She is a hired servant who assists him with his coat and has no blood relation.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Petra
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Was that not your owner, Captain Horster?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: My hat and coat, Petra!

evidence_dialogues.speaker: STAGE
evidence_dialogues.utterance: (Horn-blowing, hisses, and wild cries. DR. STOCKMANN goes out through the hall with his family, HORSTER elbowing a way for them.)

reasoning_steps.inference: Petra is part of the group identified as Dr. Stockmann's family.
The text collectively refers to those leaving with him (including Petra, Katherine, and the boys) as 'his family', confirming her familial (daughter) status.

qa.question: What formal organizational role does Aslaksen hold in relation to the townspeople?
option: D. He is the President of the Householders' Association.
E. He is the supreme commander of the local naval forces and the militia.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I refer to the President of the Householders' Association, Mr. Aslaksen.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor explicitly states Aslaksen's title, defining his formal relationship to the citizens.

qa.question: According to Dr. Stockmann's medical analogy, what is the maximum number of years a normally constituted truth typically lives?
option: E. 100 years or substantially more depending on the circumstances.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: A normally constituted truth lives, let us say, as a rule seventeen or eighteen, or at most twenty years—seldom longer.

reasoning_steps.inference: The text explicitly states the lifespan of a truth.
The absolute maximum stated in the range is 20 years.

qa.question: According to Hovstad's sarcastic remark, exactly how many days ago did Dr. Stockmann become an aristocrat?
option: E. 30 days ago or an entire month.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Hovstad
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Aha!—so Dr. Stockmann has become an aristocrat since the day before yesterday!

reasoning_steps.inference: Hovstad points to 'the day before yesterday'.
The day before yesterday is mathematically exactly 2 days ago.

qa.question: According to her husband, within how many years does a person lose their power of moral thinking in an un-aired house (like the one Katherine wants scrubbed)?
option: E. 10 to 20 years depending on the conditions.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: In a house which does not get aired and swept every day—my wife Katherine maintains that the floor ought to be scrubbed as well... in such a house, let me tell you, people will lose within two or three years the power of thinking or acting in a moral manner.

reasoning_steps.inference: Dr. Stockmann connects Katherine's cleaning rules to a specific timeline for moral decay.
He explicitly states this decay happens within 'two or three years'.

qa.question: What is the minimum amount of pounds the Mayor claims the Medical Officer's proposals would cost the ratepayers?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: ...would be to saddle the ratepayers with an unnecessary expenditure of at least some thousands of pounds.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor quotes a specific financial figure to scare the crowd.
That exact figure is 'at least some thousands of pounds'.

qa.question: Not including Captain Horster, exactly how many of Dr. Stockmann's family members are explicitly stated to walk into the room alongside or immediately following Petra at the start of the meeting?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: STAGE
evidence_dialogues.utterance: (CAPTAIN HORSTER brings in MRS. STOCKMANN and PETRA through the door on the right. EJLIF and MORTEN follow them in.)

reasoning_steps.inference: The stage direction names the people entering with Petra.
The family members listed alongside her are Mrs. Stockmann, Ejlif, and Morten (totaling 3).

qa.question: Exactly how many people present at the meeting voted against the resolution declaring Dr. Stockmann an enemy of the people?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Gentlemen, allow me to announce the result. By the votes of every one here except one person—

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: By the votes of everyone here except a tipsy man, this meeting of citizens declares Dr. Thomas Stockmann to be an enemy of the people.

reasoning_steps.inference: Aslaksen announces the mathematical result of the ballot.
He states exactly 'one person' (the tipsy man) voted against it.

session_4.speaker: NARRATION
session_4.text: (SCENE.—A big old-fashioned room in CAPTAIN HORSTER'S house. At the back folding-doors, which are standing open, lead to an ante-room. Three windows in the left-hand wall. In the middle of the opposite wall a platform has been erected. On this is a small table with two candles, a water-bottle and glass, and a bell. The room is lit by lamps placed between the windows. In the foreground on the left there is a table with candles and a chair. To the right is a door and some chairs standing near it. The room is nearly filled with a crowd of townspeople of all sorts, a few women and schoolboys being amongst them. People are still streaming in from the back, and the room is soon filled.)

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: Hullo, Lamstad! You here too?

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: I go to every public meeting, I do.

session_4.speaker: 3rd Citizen
session_4.text: Brought your whistle too, I expect!

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: I should think so. Haven't you?

session_4.speaker: 3rd Citizen
session_4.text: Rather! And old Evensen said he was going to bring a cow-horn, he did.

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: Good old Evensen!

session_4.speaker: 4th Citizen
session_4.text: I say, tell me what is going on here tonight?

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: Dr. Stockmann is going to deliver an address attacking the Mayor.

session_4.speaker: 4th Citizen
session_4.text: But the Mayor is his brother.

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: That doesn't matter; Dr. Stockmann's not the chap to be afraid.

session_4.speaker: 3rd Citizen
session_4.text: But he is in the wrong; it said so in the “People’s Messenger.”

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: Yes, I expect he must be in the wrong this time, because neither the Householders’ Association nor the Citizens’ Club would lend him their hall for his meeting.

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: He couldn’t even get the loan of the hall at the Baths.

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: No, I should think not.

session_4.speaker: A Man in another part of the crowd
session_4.text: I say—who are we to back up in this?

session_4.speaker: Another Man
session_4.text: Watch Aslaksen, and do as he does.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Excuse me, gentlemen—do you mind letting me through? I am reporting for the “People’s Messenger.” Thank you very much!

session_4.speaker: A Workman
session_4.text: Who was that?

session_4.speaker: Second Workman
session_4.text: Don’t you know him? It’s Billing, who writes for Aslaksen’s paper.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (CAPTAIN HORSTER brings in MRS. STOCKMANN and PETRA through the door on the right. EJLIF and MORTEN follow them in.)

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: I thought you might all sit here; you can slip out easily from here, if things get too lively.

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: Do you think there will be a disturbance?

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: One can never tell—with such a crowd. But sit down, and don’t be uneasy.

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: It was extremely kind of you to offer my husband the room.

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: Well, if nobody else would—

session_4.speaker: Petra
session_4.text: And it was a plucky thing to do, Captain Horster.

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: Oh, it is not such a great matter as all that.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (HOVSTAD and ASLAKSEN make their way through the crowd.)

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Has the Doctor not come yet?

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: He is waiting in the next room.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Look—here comes the Mayor!

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Yes, I’m damned if he hasn’t come after all!

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (PETER STOCKMANN makes his way gradually through the crowd, bows courteously, and takes up a position by the wall on the left. Shortly afterwards Dr. STOCKMANN comes in by the right-hand door. He is dressed in a black frock-coat, with a white tie. There is a little feeble applause, which is hushed down. Silence is obtained.)

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: How do you feel, Katherine?

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: All right, thank you. Be sure not to lose your temper, Thomas.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Oh, I know how to control myself.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Looks at his watch, steps on to the platform, and bows. It is a quarter past—so I will begin. (Takes his MS. out of his pocket).

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: I think we ought to elect a chairman first.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: No, it is quite unnecessary.

session_4.speaker: Some of the Crowd
session_4.text: Yes—yes!

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: I certainly think too that we ought to have a chairman.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: But I have called this meeting to deliver a lecture, Peter.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Dr. Stockmann’s lecture may possibly lead to a considerable conflict of opinion.

session_4.speaker: Voices in the Crowd
session_4.text: A chairman! A chairman !

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: The general wish of the meeting seems to be that a chairman should be elected.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Very well—let the meeting have its way.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Will the Mayor be good enough to undertake the task ?

session_4.speaker: Three Men
session_4.text: Bravo! Bravo!

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: For various reasons, which you will easily understand, I must beg to be excused. But fortunately we have amongst us a man who I think will be acceptable to you all. I refer to the President of the Householders' Association, Mr. Aslaksen.

session_4.speaker: Several voices
session_4.text: Yes—Aslaksen! Bravo Aslaksen!

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (DR. STOCKMANN takes up his MS. and walks up and down the platform.)

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Since my fellow-citizens choose to entrust me with this duty, I cannot refuse.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Loud applause. ASLAKSEN mounts the platform.)

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Mr. Aslaksen was elected with enthusiasm.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: And now, as I am in this position, I should like to say a few brief words. I am a quiet and peaceable man, who believes in discreet moderation, and—and—in moderate discretion. All my friends can bear witness to that.

session_4.speaker: Several Voices
session_4.text: That's right! That's right, Aslaksen!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: I have learned in the school of life and experience that moderation is the most valuable virtue a citizen can possess—

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Hear, hear!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: And moreover, that discretion and moderation are what enable a man to be of most service to the community. I would therefore suggest to our esteemed fellow-citizen, who has called this meeting, that he should strive to keep strictly within the bounds of moderation.

session_4.speaker: A Man
session_4.text: Three cheers for the Moderation Society!

session_4.speaker: A Voice
session_4.text: Shame!

session_4.speaker: Several Voices
session_4.text: Sh!-Sh!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: No interruptions, gentlemen, please! Does anyone wish to make any remarks?

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Mr. Chairman.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: The Mayor will address the meeting.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: In consideration of the close relationship in which, as you all know, I stand to the present Medical Officer of the Baths, I should have preferred not to speak this evening. But my official position with regard to the Baths and my solicitude for the vital interests of the town compel me to bring forward a motion. I venture to presume that there is not a single one of our citizens present who considers it desirable that unreliable and exaggerated accounts of the sanitary condition of the Baths and the town should be spread abroad.

session_4.speaker: Several Voices
session_4.text: No, no! Certainly not! We protest against it!

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Therefore, I should like to propose that the meeting should not permit the Medical Officer either to read or to comment on his proposed lecture.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Not permit—! What the devil—!

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: Ahem!-ahem!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Very well, Go ahead!

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: In my communication to the "People's Messenger," I have put the essential facts before the public in such a way that every fair-minded citizen can easily form his own opinion. From it you will see that the main result of the Medical Officer's proposals—apart from their constituting a vote of censure on the leading men of the town—would be to saddle the ratepayers with an unnecessary expenditure of at least some thousands of pounds.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Sounds of disapproval among the audience, and some cat-calls.)

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Silence, please, gentlemen! I beg to support the Mayor's motion. I quite agree with him that there is something behind this agitation started by the Doctor. He talks about the Baths; but it is a revolution he is aiming at—he wants to get the administration of the town put into new hands. No one doubts the honesty of the Doctor's intentions—no one will suggest that there can be any two opinions as to that, I myself am a believer in self-government for the people, provided it does not fall too heavily on the ratepayers. But that would be the case here; and that is why I will see Dr. Stockmann damned—I beg your pardon—before I go with him in the matter. You can pay too dearly for a thing sometimes; that is my opinion.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Loud applause on all sides.)

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: I, too, feel called upon to explain my position. Dr. Stockmann's agitation appeared to be gaining a certain amount of sympathy at first, so I supported it as impartially as I could. But presently we had reason to suspect that we had allowed ourselves to be misled by misrepresentation of the state of affairs—

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Misrepresentation—!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Well, let us say a not entirely trustworthy representation. The Mayor's statement has proved that. I hope no one here has any doubt as to my liberal principles; the attitude of the "People's Messenger" towards important political questions is well known to everyone. But the advice of experienced and thoughtful men has convinced me that in purely local matters a newspaper ought to proceed with a certain caution.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: I entirely agree with the speaker.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: And, in the matter before us, it is now an undoubted fact that Dr. Stockmann has public opinion against him. Now, what is an editor's first and most obvious duty, gentlemen? Is it not to work in harmony with his readers? Has he not received a sort of tacit mandate to work persistently and assiduously for the welfare of those whose opinions he represents? Or is it possible I am mistaken in that?

session_4.speaker: Voices from the crowd
session_4.text: No, no! You are quite right!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: It has cost me a severe struggle to break with a man in whose house I have been lately a frequent guest—a man who till today has been able to pride himself on the undivided goodwill of his fellow-citizens—a man whose only, or at all events whose essential, failing is that he is swayed by his heart rather than his head.

session_4.speaker: A few scattered voices
session_4.text: That is true! Bravo, Stockmann!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: But my duty to the community obliged me to break with him. And there is another consideration that impels me to oppose him, and, as far as possible, to arrest him on the perilous course he has adopted; that is, consideration for his family—

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Please stick to the water-supply and drainage!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: consideration, I repeat, for his wife and his children for whom he has made no provision.

session_4.speaker: Morten
session_4.text: Is that us, mother?

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: Hush!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: I will now put the Mayor's proposition to the vote.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: There is no necessity! Tonight I have no intention of dealing with all that filth down at the Baths. No; I have something quite different to say to you.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: What is coming now?

session_4.speaker: A Drunken Man
session_4.text: I am a ratepayer! And therefore, I have a right to speak too! And my entire—firm—inconceivable opinion is—

session_4.speaker: A number of voices
session_4.text: Be quiet, at the back there!

session_4.speaker: Others
session_4.text: He is drunk! Turn him out!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Am I allowed to speak?

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Dr. Stockmann will address the meeting.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I should like to have seen anyone, a few days ago, dare to attempt to silence me as has been done tonight! I would have defended my sacred rights as a man, like a lion! But now it is all one to me; I have something of even weightier importance to say to you.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I have thought and pondered a great deal, these last few days—pondered over such a variety of things that in the end my head seemed too full to hold them—

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Ahem!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: —but I got them clear in my mind at last, and then I saw the whole situation lucidly. And that is why I am standing here to-night. I have a great revelation to make to you, my fellow-citizens! I will impart to you a discovery of a far wider scope than the trifling matter that our water supply is poisoned and our medicinal Baths are standing on pestiferous soil.

session_4.speaker: A number of voices
session_4.text: Don't talk about the Baths! We won't hear you! None of that!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I have already told you that what I want to speak about is the great discovery I have made lately—the discovery that all the sources of our moral life are poisoned and that the whole fabric of our civic community is founded on the pestiferous soil of falsehood.

session_4.speaker: Voices of disconcerted Citizens
session_4.text: What is that he says?

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Such an insinuation—!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: I call upon the speaker to moderate his language.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I have always loved my native town as a man only can love the home of his youthful days. I was not old when I went away from here; and exile, longing and memories cast as it were an additional halo over both the town and its inhabitants. And there I stayed, for many years, in a horrible hole far away up north. When I came into contact with some of the people that lived scattered about among the rocks, I often thought it would of been more service to the poor half-starved creatures if a veterinary doctor had been sent up there, instead of a man like me.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: I'm damned if I have ever heard—!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: It is an insult to a respectable population!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Wait a bit! I do not think anyone will charge me with having forgotten my native town up there. I was like one of the eider-ducks brooding on its nest, and what I hatched was the plans for these Baths. And then when fate at last decreed for me the great happiness of coming home again—I assure you, gentlemen, I thought I had nothing more in the world to wish for. Or rather, there was one thing I wished for—eagerly, untiringly, ardently—and that was to be able to be of service to my native town and the good of the community.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: You chose a strange way of doing it—ahem!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: And so, with my eyes blinded to the real facts, I revelled in happiness. But yesterday morning—no, to be precise, it was yesterday afternoon—the eyes of my mind were opened wide, and the first thing I realised was the colossal stupidity of the authorities—.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Mr. Chairman!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: By virtue of my authority—!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: It is a petty thing to catch me up on a word, Mr. Aslaksen. What I mean is only that I got scent of the unbelievable piggishness our leading men had been responsible for down at the Baths. I can't stand leading men at any price!—I have had enough of such people in my time. They are like billy-goats on a young plantation; they do mischief everywhere. They stand in a free man's way, whichever way he turns, and what I should like best would be to see them exterminated like any other vermin—.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Mr. Chairman, can we allow such expressions to pass?

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Doctor—!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I cannot understand how it is that I have only now acquired a clear conception of what these gentry are, when I had almost daily before my eyes in this town such an excellent specimen of them—my brother Peter—slow-witted and hide-bound in prejudice—.

session_4.speaker: The Drunken Man
session_4.text: Is it me he is talking about?

session_4.speaker: Angry Voices
session_4.text: Turn out that drunken man! Turn him out.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Who was that person?

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: I don't know who he is, Mr. Mayor.

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: He doesn't belong here.

session_4.speaker: 3rd Citizen
session_4.text: I expect he is a navvy from over at—(the rest is inaudible).

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: He had obviously had too much beer. Proceed, Doctor; but please strive to be moderate in your language.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Very well, gentlemen, I will say no more about our leading men. And if anyone imagines, from what I have just said, that my object is to attack these people this evening, he is wrong—absolutely wide of the mark. For I cherish the comforting conviction that these parasites—all these venerable relics of a dying school of thought—are most admirably paving the way for their own extinction; they need no doctor's help to hasten their end. Nor is it folk of that kind who constitute the most pressing danger to the community. It is not they who are most instrumental in poisoning the sources of our moral life and infecting the ground on which we stand. It is not they who are the most dangerous enemies of truth and freedom amongst us.

session_4.speaker: UNKNOWN
session_4.text: Who then? Who is it? Name! Name!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: You may depend upon it—I shall name them! That is precisely the great discovery I made yesterday. The most dangerous enemy of truth and freedom amongst us is the compact majority—yes, the damned compact Liberal majority—that is it! Now you know!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: As Chairman, I call upon the speaker to withdraw the ill-considered expressions he has just used.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Never, Mr. Aslaksen! It is the majority in our community that denies me my freedom and seeks to prevent my speaking the truth.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: The majority always has right on its side.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: And truth too, by God!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: The majority never has right on its side. Never, I say! That is one of these social lies against which an independent, intelligent man must wage war. Who is it that constitute the majority of the population in a country? Is it the clever folk, or the stupid? I don't imagine you will dispute the fact that at present the stupid people are in an absolutely overwhelming majority all the world over. But, good Lord!—you can never pretend that it is right that the stupid folk should govern the clever ones! Oh, yes—you can shout me down, I know! But you cannot answer me. The majority has might on its side—unfortunately; but right it has not. I am in the right—I and a few other scattered individuals. The minority is always in the right.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Aha!—so Dr. Stockmann has become an aristocrat since the day before yesterday!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I have already said that I don't intend to waste a word on the puny, narrow-chested, short-winded crew whom we are leaving astern. Pulsating life no longer concerns itself with them. I am thinking of the few, the scattered few amongst us, who have absorbed new and vigorous truths. Such men stand, as it were, at the outposts, so far ahead that the compact majority has not yet been able to come up with them; and there they are fighting for truths that are too newly-born into the world of consciousness to have any considerable number of people on their side as yet.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: So the Doctor is a revolutionary now!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Good heavens—of course I am, Mr. Hovstad! I propose to raise a revolution against the lie that the majority has the monopoly of the truth. What sort of truths are they that the majority usually supports? They are truths that are of such advanced age that they are beginning to break up. And if a truth is as old as that, it is also in a fair way to become a lie, gentlemen. Yes, believe me or not, as you like; but truths are by no means as long-lived at Methuselah—as some folk imagine. A normally constituted truth lives, let us say, as a rule seventeen or eighteen, or at most twenty years—seldom longer. But truths as aged as that are always worn frightfully thin, and nevertheless it is only then that the majority recognises them and recommends them to the community as wholesome moral nourishment. There is no great nutritive value in that sort of fare, I can assure you; and, as a doctor, I ought to know. These "majority truths" are like last year's cured meat—like rancid, tainted ham; and they are the origin of the moral scurvy that is rampant in our communities.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: It appears to me that the speaker is wandering a long way from his subject.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: I quite agree with the Chairman.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Have you gone clean out of your senses, Peter? I am sticking as closely to my subject as I can; for my subject is precisely this, that it is the masses, the majority—this infernal compact majority—that poisons the sources of our moral life and infects the ground we stand on.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: And all this because the great, broadminded majority of the people is prudent enough to show deference only to well-ascertained and well-approved truths?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Ah, my good Mr. Hovstad, don't talk nonsense about well-ascertained truths! The truths of which the masses now approve are the very truths that the fighters at the outposts held to in the days of our grandfathers. We fighters at the outposts nowadays no longer approve of them; and I do not believe there is any other well-ascertained truth except this, that no community can live a healthy life if it is nourished only on such old marrowless truths.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: But, instead of standing there using vague generalities, it would be interesting if you would tell us what these old marrowless truths are, that we are nourished on.

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Applause from many quarters.)

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Oh, I could give you a whole string of such abominations; but to begin with I will confine myself to one well-approved truth, which at bottom is a foul lie, but upon which nevertheless Mr. Hovstad and the "People's Messenger" and all the "Messenger's" supporters are nourished.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: And that is—?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: That is, the doctrine you have inherited from your forefathers and proclaim thoughtlessly far and wide—the doctrine that the public, the crowd, the masses, are the essential part of the population—that they constitute the People—that the common folk, the ignorant and incomplete element in the community, have the same right to pronounce judgment and to approve, to direct and to govern, as the isolated, intellectually superior personalities in it.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Well, damn me if ever I—

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Fellow-citizens, take good note of that!

session_4.speaker: A number of voices
session_4.text: Oho!—we are not the People! Only the superior folk are to govern, are they!

session_4.speaker: A Workman
session_4.text: Turn the fellow out for talking such rubbish!

session_4.speaker: Another
session_4.text: Out with him!

session_4.speaker: Another
session_4.text: Blow your horn, Evensen!

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (A horn is blown loudly, amidst hisses and an angry uproar.)

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Be reasonable! Can't you stand hearing the voice of truth for once? I don't in the least expect you to agree with me all at once; but I must say I did expect Mr. Hovstad to admit I was right, when he had recovered his composure a little. He claims to be a freethinker—

session_4.speaker: Voices
session_4.text: Freethinker, did he say? Is Hovstad a freethinker?

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Prove it, Dr. Stockmann! When have I said so in print?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: No, confound it, you are right!—you have never had the courage to. Well, I won't put you in a hole, Mr. Hovstad. Let us say it is I that am the freethinker, then. I am going to prove to you, scientifically, that the "People's Messenger" leads you by the nose in a shameful manner when it tells you that you—that the common people, the crowd, the masses, are the real essence of the People. That is only a newspaper lie, I tell you! The common people are nothing more than the raw material of which a People is made. Well, isn't that the case? Isn't there an enormous difference between a well-bred and an ill-bred strain of animals? Take, for instance, a common barn-door hen. What sort of eating do you get from a shrivelled up old scrag of a fowl like that? Not much, do you! And what sort of eggs does it lay? A fairly good crow or a raven can lay pretty nearly as good an egg. But take a well-bred Spanish or Japanese hen, or a good pheasant or a turkey—then you will see the difference. Or take the case of dogs, with whom we humans are on such intimate terms. Think first of an ordinary common cur—I mean one of the horrible, coarse-haired, low-bred curs that do nothing but run about the streets and befoul the walls of the houses. Compare one of these curs with a poodle whose sires for many generations have been bred in a gentleman's house, where they have had the best of food and had the opportunity of hearing soft voices and music. Do you not think that the poodle's brain is developed to quite a different degree from that of the cur? Of course it is. It is puppies of well-bred poodles like that, that showmen train to do incredibly clever tricks—things that a common cur could never learn to do even if it stood on its head.

session_4.speaker: A Citizen
session_4.text: Are you going to make out we are dogs, now?

session_4.speaker: Another Citizen
session_4.text: We are not animals, Doctor!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Yes but, bless my soul, we are, my friend! It is true we are the finest animals anyone could wish for; but, even among us, exceptionally fine animals are rare. There is a tremendous difference between poodle-men and cur-men. And the amusing part of it is, that Mr. Hovstad quite agrees with me as long as it is a question of four-footed animals—

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Yes, it is true enough as far as they are concerned.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Very well. But as soon as I extend the principle and apply it to two-legged animals, Mr. Hovstad stops short. He no longer dares to think independently, or to pursue his ideas to their logical conclusion; so, he turns the whole theory upside down and proclaims in the "People's Messenger" that it is the barn-door hens and street curs that are the finest specimens in the menagerie. But that is always the way, as long as a man retains the traces of common origin and has not worked his way up to intellectual distinction.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: I lay no claim to any sort of distinction, I am the son of humble country-folk, and I am proud that the stock I come from is rooted deep among the common people he insults.

session_4.speaker: Voices
session_4.text: Bravo, Hovstad! Bravo! Bravo!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: The kind of common people I mean are not only to be found low down in the social scale; they crawl and swarm all around us—even in the highest social positions. You have only to look at your own fine, distinguished Mayor! My brother Peter is every bit as plebeian as anyone that walks in two shoes—

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: I protest against personal allusions of this kind.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: and that, not because he is like myself, descended from some old rascal of a pirate from Pomerania or thereabouts—because that is who we are descended from—

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: An absurd legend. I deny it!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: —but because he thinks what his superiors think, and holds the same opinions as they, People who do that are, intellectually speaking, common people; and, that is why my magnificent brother Peter is in reality so very far from any distinction—and consequently also so far from being liberal-minded.

session_4.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_4.text: Mr. Chairman—!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: So it is only the distinguished men that are liberal-minded in this country? We are learning something quite new!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Yes, that is part of my new discovery too. And another part of it is that broad-mindedness is almost precisely the same thing as morality. That is why I maintain that it is absolutely inexcusable in the "People's Messenger" to proclaim, day in and day out, the false doctrine that it is the masses, the crowd, the compact majority, that have the monopoly of broad-mindedness and morality—and that vice and corruption and every kind of intellectual depravity are the result of culture, just as all the filth that is draining into our Baths is the result of the tanneries up at Molledal! And yet this same "People's Messenger" can go on preaching that the masses ought to be elevated to higher conditions of life! But, bless my soul, if the "Messenger's" teaching is to be depended upon, this very raising up the masses would mean nothing more or less than setting them straightway upon the paths of depravity! Happily the theory that culture demoralises is only an old falsehood that our forefathers believed in and we have inherited. No, it is ignorance, poverty, ugly conditions of life, that do the devil's work! In a house which does not get aired and swept every day—my wife Katherine maintains that the floor ought to be scrubbed as well, but that is a debatable question—in such a house, let me tell you, people will lose within two or three years the power of thinking or acting in a moral manner. Lack of oxygen weakens the conscience. And there must be a plentiful lack of oxygen in very many houses in this town, I should think, judging from the fact that the whole compact majority can be unconscientious enough to wish to build the town's prosperity on a quagmire of falsehood and deceit.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: We cannot allow such a grave accusation to be flung at a citizen community.

session_4.speaker: A Citizen
session_4.text: I move that the Chairman direct the speaker to sit down.

session_4.speaker: Voices
session_4.text: Hear, hear! Quite right! Make him sit down!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Then I will go and shout the truth at every street corner! I will write it in other towns' newspapers! The whole country shall know what is going on here!

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: It almost seems as if Dr. Stockmann's intention were to ruin the town.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Yes, my native town is so dear to me that I would rather ruin it than see it flourishing upon a lie.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: This is really serious.

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: A man must be a public enemy to wish to ruin a whole community!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: What does the destruction of a community matter, if it lives on lies? It ought to be razed to the ground. I tell you— All who live by lies ought to be exterminated like vermin! You will end by infecting the whole country; you will bring about such a state of things that the whole country will deserve to be ruined. And if things come to that pass, I shall say from the bottom of my heart: Let the whole country perish, let all these people be exterminated!

session_4.speaker: Voices from the crowd
session_4.text: That is talking like an out-and-out enemy of the people!

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: There sounded the voice of the people, by all that's holy!

session_4.speaker: The whole crowd
session_4.text: Yes, yes! He is an enemy of the people! He hates his country! He hates his own people!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Both as a citizen and as an individual, I am profoundly disturbed by what we have had to listen to. Dr. Stockmann has shown himself in a light I should never have dreamed of. I am unhappily obliged to subscribe to the opinion which I have just heard my estimable fellow-citizens utter; and I propose that we should give expression to that opinion in a resolution. I propose a resolution as follows: "This meeting declares that it considers Dr. Thomas Stockmann, Medical Officer of the Baths, to be an enemy of the people."

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Oh, you fools! I tell you that—

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: We cannot hear you now, Doctor. A formal vote is about to be taken; but, out of regard for personal feelings, it shall be by ballot and not verbal. Have you any clean paper, Mr. Billing?

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: I have both blue and white here.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: That will do nicely; we shall get on more quickly that way. Cut it up into small strips—yes, that's it. Blue means no; white means yes. I will come round myself and collect votes.

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: I say, what has come to the Doctor? What are we to think of it?

session_4.speaker: Hovstad
session_4.text: Oh, you know how headstrong he is.

session_4.speaker: 2nd Citizen
session_4.text: Billing, you go to their house—have you ever noticed if the fellow drinks?

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Well I'm hanged if I know what to say. There are always spirits on the table when you go.

session_4.speaker: 3rd Citizen
session_4.text: I rather think he goes quite off his head sometimes.

session_4.speaker: 1st Citizen
session_4.text: I wonder if there is any madness in his family?

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: I shouldn't wonder if there were.

session_4.speaker: 4th Citizen
session_4.text: No, it is nothing more than sheer malice; he wants to get even with somebody for something or other.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Well certainly he suggested a rise in his salary on one occasion lately, and did not get it.

session_4.speaker: The Citizens
session_4.text: Ah!—then it is easy to understand how it is!

session_4.speaker: The Drunken Man
session_4.text: I want a blue one, I do! And I want a white one too!

session_4.speaker: Voices
session_4.text: It's that drunken chap again! Turn him out!

session_4.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_4.text: Well, Stockmann, do you see what these monkey tricks of yours lead to?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I have done my duty.

session_4.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_4.text: What was that you said about the tanneries at Molledal?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: You heard well enough. I said they were the source of all the filth.

session_4.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_4.text: My tannery too?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Unfortunately your tannery is by far the worst.

session_4.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_4.text: Are you going to put that in the papers?

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: I shall conceal nothing.

session_4.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_4.text: That may cost you dearly, Stockmann.

session_4.speaker: A Stout Man
session_4.text: Well, Captain, so you lend your house to enemies of the people?

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: I imagine I can do what I like with my own possessions, Mr. Vik.

session_4.speaker: The Stout Man
session_4.text: Then you can have no objection to my doing the same with mine.

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: What do you mean, sir?

session_4.speaker: The Stout Man
session_4.text: You shall hear from me in the morning.

session_4.speaker: Petra
session_4.text: Was that not your owner, Captain Horster?

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: Yes, that was Mr. Vik the shipowner.

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: Gentlemen, allow me to announce the result. By the votes of every one here except one person—

session_4.speaker: A Young Man
session_4.text: That is the drunk chap!

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: By the votes of everyone here except a tipsy man, this meeting of citizens declares Dr. Thomas Stockmann to be an enemy of the people. Three cheers for our ancient and honourable citizen community! Three cheers for our able and energetic Mayor, who has so loyally suppressed the promptings of family feeling! The meeting is dissolved.

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Three cheers for the Chairman!

session_4.speaker: The whole crowd
session_4.text: Three cheers for Aslaksen! Hurrah!

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: My hat and coat, Petra! Captain, have you room on your ship for passengers to the New World?

session_4.speaker: Horster
session_4.text: For you and yours we will make room, Doctor.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: Good. Come, Katherine! Come, boys!

session_4.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_4.text: Thomas, dear, let us go out by the back way.

session_4.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_4.text: No back ways for me, Katherine, You will hear more of this enemy of the people, before he shakes the dust off his shoes upon you! I am not so forgiving as a certain Person; I do not say: "I forgive you, for ye know not what ye do."

session_4.speaker: Aslaksen
session_4.text: That is a blasphemous comparison, Dr. Stockmann!

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: It is, by God! It's dreadful for an earnest man to listen to.

session_4.speaker: A Coarse Voice
session_4.text: Threatens us now, does he!

session_4.speaker: Other Voices
session_4.text: Let's go and break his windows! Duck him in the fjord!

session_4.speaker: Another Voice
session_4.text: Blow your horn, Evensen! Pip, pip!

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (Horn-blowing, hisses, and wild cries. DR. STOCKMANN goes out through the hall with his family, HORSTER elbowing a way for them.)

session_4.speaker: The Whole Crowd
session_4.text:
Enemy of the People!
Enemy of the People!

session_4.speaker: Billing
session_4.text: Well, I'm damned if I go and drink toddy with the Stockmanns tonight!

session_4.speaker: STAGE
session_4.text: (The crowd press towards the exit. The uproar continues outside; shouts of "Enemy of the People!" are heard from without.)
